speaker: interviewer
text: New York, on February 22, 199-- I'm sorry. February 5, 1995. I think where we left off on the last tape was you were talking about your experience and awareness of anti-Semitism in your childhood.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mhm. Yes. I began talking about the church across the street from us. Every Easter, as I recall-- and there were not that many. I don't want to exaggerate, because we didn't live there that long-- on Nowolipki Street. The processions used to produce a lot of anti-Semitic activity. Kids used to throw rocks at us. And I mentioned before, I don't know whether I made it clear, men's beards were shaved. But this was not necessarily during that period alone that these religious men's beards were shaved. Sometimes half the face. And, of course, the Germans did it also, and in a much more cruel way than the Poles did at the time. I was in a hospital as a child, I had an appendectomy. I think I was about six or seven years old. And it was a Jewish clinic, actually. And there was a Polish boy who made my life miserable because I was Jewish. And he taunted me for my Jewishness.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it's very difficult. You know, we just took it for granted. It's difficult for you to understand, but it was just part of life. And I was not surprised. And I suppose if I had been here and experienced it, I would have been much more shocked because one is not accustomed to that anti-Semitism.

speaker: interviewee
text: But ultimately, you integrate it into your consciousness. You live with it, because you have no choice. And you make the best of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you make the best of it? You retain your sense of humanity. You retain your sense of self-hood, which was extremely important later in the ghetto and concentration camps. You never allow yourself to be dehumanized. And that's how you fight back, because you cannot fight back with rocks, certainly not children.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was extremely dangerous to fight back, although there were many resistance movements in Poland. After the First World War, there was a powerful resistance movement. I don't know, I'm not too familiar with precisely what they did, but I know that they, you know, organized themselves to fight back. And it was very difficult, because Jews didn't have quite the same recourse to law, as I remember it. I haven't studied it, and I may perhaps not present it in the most accurate way. But as I knew it, Jews didn't have the same kind of recourse to the courts of law, to law in general, as the Poles. You may know the fact that when a Jew went to the university, even to high school, he had to bring a Gentile with him, otherwise he would not be admitted to the university, or even to high school. But that was before. That was before the period during which I was born. But I really don't know-- during which I lived, I should say. But I don't really know what the nature of education was. I think more Jews were allowed a greater freedom to go to school without having to bring a Polish student with them. I don't know whether it's clear to you what I mean to bring-- to make sure that a Polish student gets also an education.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was incumbent upon the Jewish student to make sure that he encourages a Polish one to go, you know, to college or high school, as far as I know. I know this to be a fact. I mean, this is not hearsay. I just don't know the precise time, what it was. I don't think it was during the time that I was growing up, but I think it was before. So certainly, this is not an area of expertise for me. What were some of the first signs to you that anti-Semitism and danger to Jews were escalating?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1939-- '36, '37, German Jews were expelled from Germany. Not all of them, but those Jews who had been born in Poland, who emigrated to Germany, were sent back to Poland. So that were the first signs. Among the first signs were also-- I remember my parents were glued to the radio always-- speeches of Hitler that they were able to catch on the radio. And the anti-Semitism in Poland was encouraged by virtue of what was going on in Germany. There were many anti-Semites in Poland, which is not to say that there were not many decent Poles, you know. I don't want to smear everyone with the same brush. I'm sure there were some decent Poles. Some saved Jews here and there. Not too many, which shows that more could have done it. You know, if a few were able to save, more could have done it, too.

speaker: interviewee
text: So it seems to me that I was aware of anti-Semitism virtually since I was born. It was an integral part of our existence. You know, Jews are not equal to Christians, you know. Although we always thought the other way around. You know, we always felt that we're OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Nonetheless, it is very difficult to extricate yourself from this notion that is constantly pressed upon you that you are not as good as the others. So there was a measure of shame in being Jewish, too. For example, if you were blonde, it was a boon, but it was also a-- and it was also a compliment. Even now, when I speak sometimes with my survivor friends, they say, well, they could not recognize me because I had blonde hair and blue eyes, as if it were-- as if it were a compliment.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's, in some ways, much the way it is with every Afro-Americans who are also often ashamed of who they are, because, you know, it's better to be light than to be dark. And in fact, I tell my students that if I were black, I'd want to be the blackest black, because why should I be ashamed of who I am? I've learned this, you know, in the course of all the years of suffering and growing up and getting to be sixty, seventy years old. So I was always aware of anti-Semitism. It was just an integral part of our existence, but it was obviously-- your question, I'm not sure I answered clearly-- it was much more-- much more pervasive and much keener. I mean, we felt it in a much more keen way.

speaker: interviewee
text: For example, the shaving of the beards, and the-- I understand that some Jews had their ears cut, you know, and the beatings, it became progressively more frequent. So it was-- the atmosphere was permeated with it. I mean, one breathed it. So in some way, we were prepared in a kind of ironic-- bitterly ironic way we were prepared for what was to come. Of course, we didn't know, but it was like a kind of foreshadowing, a forerunner, of what was to come. But, of course, nobody could read the stars. Nobody could understand. Nobody could imagine. I mean, for the last moment until 1943, January 1943, I didn't know there were gas chambers. This was just a few-- a couple of months before the Warsaw Ghetto Uprising. So we lived-- but we'll talk about it probably a little later.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, what were the first direct effects of the war on you and your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, first was the bombing. And we hid, initially, before my father fled to Russia or to the Soviet Union--

speaker: interviewer
text: For that week?

speaker: interviewee
text: For that week, right. We hid in a basement apartment. People live in basement apartments. And then, when my father left, we went to my aunt's house who lived on a ground floor. This is the aunt whose children were David and Yosek, or Joseph. And we stayed with them until my father came back. And the bombing was just relentless. And much of Warsaw I was destroyed. Obviously, not as much as during the uprising. I'm not talking about the Jewish uprising, but the Polish uprising. You may know that there was an uprising after the uprising of the Warsaw Ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: So much of Warsaw was destroyed, but, fortunately, our apartment house, that is the tenement house in which we lived, was not touched nor were those of my aunts. You know, they also were able to return home. And then, when the Germans entered-- well, maybe I should mention what I wanted to mention about my father. Only about eight years ago, something like that, I don't remember exactly. I remember being in the kitchen and thinking of my father, and I felt tremendous anger. It was anger and such anguish that I find it difficult to describe, because, for the first time, I came face-to-face with the fact that he left us. It was a terrible, terrible feeling, and also one that was permeated with guilt, because how could I feel angry at this angel who did so much for us? And I'll tell you what he did for us.

speaker: interviewee
text: And, ultimately, you know, I made peace with this. But in some way, it was a kind of catharsis. You know, the anger that I felt as a child that was repressed kind of emerged and sort of spilled over for a couple of days. It's not that I was dysfunctional. I functioned quite well. I was teaching, I was doing everything that I had to do, but there was pain. There was mostly pain. And I spoke with my sister about it. And she never experienced this. And she's an extremely sensitive person and also one who is keenly aware of herself. That is to say, her feelings. She didn't feel this way. In some way, she's wiser than I. And I'm not saying it just to praise her. She really is an extraordinary woman. And she somehow-- although she's younger than I, and she was younger at the time, she was able to integrate it better than I did. But anyway, it was a kind of purging for me. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: About the fact that he had left to go to Russia?

speaker: interviewee
text: He left for-- yes. And then he came back. And then, we had to give up our treasured possessions, such as jewelry. The Germans demanded that we give up our furs and jewelry, not that we had any furs, but we had some jewelry, especially since my father was a jeweler. But, obviously, nobody gave up everything. You know, Jewish people are not fools. Nor are other people fools. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And then all the schools were closed, all the businesses were closed, but there were-- then stores did open. In fact, there were cafes a little bit later. And culture thrived, believe it or not, in the Warsaw Ghetto. I don't know whether or not you're aware of it. It was all underground. There were concerts, there were readings of poetry, there was a great deal of writing, a great deal of art was produced. Certainly in the beginning, but even later. And my father made sure that my sister and I received a good education. So-- he was not the only one-- but there were groups of children, they were called [NON-ENGLISH], which means a group of children. There were five of us in the group to which I belong. Five children, we had five teachers, and we studied in each other's homes, every day in a different house. So I had--

speaker: interviewer
text: In the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the ghetto. So I completed five years of elementary school before the war, and then I did the sixth grade, you know, in the clandestine school, in an underground school, and two years of gymnasium, of high school. Learned Latin, you know, and Hebrew-- because we had a Hebrew teacher, which was great, because we had already had four years of Hebrew anyway-- and everything else. And it was extremely dangerous. We had to carry our books under our coats, because if we're caught, we would have been shot on the spot. So I think that this is extremely important to note, because this, in some way, reflects the forms of moral, or, if you will, spiritual resistance. When I speak about spiritual resistance, I don't mean anything very lofty, you know, anything very religious. Not at all, in fact. It's not religious at all. It's rather a kind of way of resisting total destruction. And I think, for us Jews, it was always the best way, because we didn't have weapons, we were always second-class citizens in whatever country we lived, you know. We lived in one country, we're expelled from it over a period of hundreds of years, and then invited to another one and expelled. So we're always ready, you know, to leave. And the only thing, the only possession, that was permanent was education. You know, we had it here. We carried all our possessions here, which was extremely important. And perhaps also here, in the soul.

speaker: interviewee
text: So for us, it was extremely significant. The libraries, there were libraries in the Warsaw Ghetto. And, as a matter of fact, both my sister and I performed in a theater for children. And it was all underground. What do I mean by 'underground'? I don't mean a bunker. I mean that we did it so that nobody would know. Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know. And we had on our street a schoolhouse-- when the ghetto was closed, but I'll talk about it in a minute-- where we-- I think we once had a very ambitious performance. All children-- it was a play for children-- it was a story about Katie who lost her geese. I'll say it in Polish. [POLISH] And I played the lead role, and my sister played also an important role. And there were two Jewish actors who never identified as Jews, but they were catapulted into the ghetto anyway. Michal, Michael, Michal Znicz, and Irena, which is a woman, Irena Brezka. And they were the ones who guided us, who taught us. And they promised me and my mother that, if we survived the war, they'll train me, because I was so gifted. Well, they told it to everyone. Every child was told that he or she was gifted, and they'll train them. I found out about it later through, you know, sort of-- not directly. And I'm sure they did it anyway, because the troupe kept our morale up. You know, all of these things were extremely important.

speaker: interviewer
text: So initially, the conditions were not as bad, but they became progressively worse. You may know that, until 1942, the Germans did not intend to gas the Jewish people. That is to say, they were not-- they did not intend to send them into the gas chambers-- in Majdanek, where I was, Auschwitz, Sobibor, Treblinka, and the others-- but, ultimately, this was their decision. And things became just absolutely awful.

speaker: interviewee
text: But even in the beginning, they would round up people-- the Germans, Lithuanians were notorious. The Lithuanians were extremely cruel. Really, they were bestial. And the Lithuanians, Ukrainians, used round up people-- pick people up from the street, put them onto a truck and send them off. We didn't know where. And the irony and the tragedy of it is that these people were made to write letters to us from wherever they, they said they were sent east, and everything is fine. They were much better off than in Warsaw, because the starvation at this point-- in the beginning, already-- the starvation was really awful. And I want to talk about it some more, not only about the starvation, but the influx of people from neighboring towns, from all over in Poland and Europe, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. Actually, let's back up just a little bit to your entry into the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh. OK. I didn't finish my thought, but I don't even remember exactly what it was. I guess it doesn't matter. We were very fortunate in that our building was incorporated into the ghetto. In other words, the ghetto-- our building was in the ghetto, because we lived in Jewish quarters. So in that, we were extremely fortunate because we did not have to give up our apartment. So we had some roots still. But neighbors-- not neighbors, relatives of my mother, who lived in a small town not too far from Warsaw, were expelled from their little-- it was almost a village or a tiny town, a shtetl, and they moved in with us. And at one point, there may have been about 18 people in this small apartment that was larger than on Mila Street, but it was still small. And then I don't remember what happened to them. They just disappeared. You know, one by one, or maybe they moved. I don't remember with precision. I could make up something, but I don't want to. You know, I want to be honest about it. So they just disappeared.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, yeah. What were the conditions? People from all over Poland, that is Jewish people from various shtetls, villages, towns, were made to come to Poland. And there were no horses. And, of course, who had a car? Very few people had cars. And so they were dragging their possessions in wagons. And the people were the mules and the horses, you know, pushing and dragging them.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there was not enough-- the ghetto was very, very small. I don't remember precisely how many streets or how large the ghetto was. Actually, I should have looked it up, because I have the information, but I didn't think of it. And there was no room for them. So there was living in the streets. And winters in Poland are very, very bitter. And there was no food for them. So they were just dying. I mean, the streets were strewn with corpses. And one sort of had to step over them. And I saw something and Channel 13 the other day about the Holocaust, and the Poles, I believe, or the Germans, I don't remember-- no, I think it was the Germans, took footage of it, made a film, and they showed how indifferent the Jews were to the Jews. Those Jews who still had fairly good clothes-- you know, like, we still had relatively good clothes, we were dressed warmly, because we had our apartment.

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course we stepped over them. What choice did we have? It's not that we're indifferent, but there wasn't a thing we could do. We all agonized over it. I remember they picked up the corpses, there were special people who picked up the corpses, put them onto wagons, drew them to the cemetery.

speaker: interviewee
text: And of course we grieved, and we tore our hair out-- not literally-- but there wasn't a thing we could do because all our apartments, everybody, took in people, as many as possible. And it reaches a point where you cannot really share the apartment with more people than you already are sharing it with.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you participated in school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in plays.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And dance, also.

speaker: interviewer
text: And dance.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. There was a dance teacher in our building, and she gave us lessons. I don't know if we paid her for doing that. But, yeah, we did ballet, my sister and I. That was in the beginning. And then many people moved into the building who shared apartments with their relatives, and some of these children initially were involved in this, too, in the dancing. In some way, we were better off than a great many people, largely because we had our apartment. But ultimately, we were-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] we had to leave the apartment. I don't know which order to go. Let me go in some chronological order what happened. When the roundups became more frequent, it was more and more difficult to leave your house. Initially, the roundups were largely from the street, in the streets. Later, in 1942, in July, 1942, the decree to murder all the Jews came, and so things changed very, very dramatically. It was in July, as I believe I said. And at that point, the Germans, the Ukrainians, the Lithuanians-- maybe even before, but I don't remember that as clearly as I remember this-- came into the homes of the people and just picked up the people and took them. And if you tried to fight or to resist, you were shot on the spot. We had no weapons.

speaker: interviewer
text: Many people don't understand. They say, why didn't you fight back? With what? We could only fight back with our spirit, with our, you know, moral strength. That was the only weapon we had. There were-- as a matter of fact, there were-- I'm going to backtrack, if I may-- there were prayer houses and there were soup kitchens. There were many self-help organizations.

speaker: interviewee
text: There were orphanages for the children. I don't know if you ever heard about Janusz Korczak, whom we saw all the time. He was a physician, and he also was a writer. And he was in the orphanage with the children. And then, ultimately, he was led to the gas chamber with the children. Even though people pleaded with him to leave the Warsaw Ghetto-- he could have. Somebody wanted to give him an opportunity to leave the ghetto, but he wouldn't. He went with the children. Anyway, I'm sorry that I'm so distracted. You know, the images crowd my head. So things became progressively worse. And after July, 1942, it was so bad that we had to find a hiding place for us.

speaker: interviewee
text: So what we did, we lived on the third floor-- in a third floor apartment. And the apartments, you know, the same apartments correspond to each other on the same side. So there was one room where my father worked that was covered with a chifferobe, you know, a closet. We didn't have built-in closets. It was covered with that, and this is where we hid.

speaker: interviewee
text: But all the apartments, all the corresponding apartments, did the same thing. The same trick was played. You know, we put some-- covered that wall. It was a kind of ruse to pretend that this is all that there is. There's no other room. So we were very fortunate to escape many of the roundups. And, in fact, when we studied, we studied every day in-- at this point, we didn't study anymore. It was impossible to go to school. But my father read to us Yiddish books. Every night, he read to us Yiddish books of the great writers like Peretz, Shalom Aleichem, and all the others, actually, throughout the period. Even though we're not religious, our sense of Jewishness was very profound, you know, and very intact.

speaker: interviewer
text: So anyway, to go back to this hiding room. So all the rooms that corresponded had the same-- Consistently.

speaker: interviewee
text: Something like it. Something to cover that wall. And one day, somebody, I think it was a Ukrainian, to whom it occurred that it was strange that all the rooms on that particular wall had a large piece of furniture, like a chifferobe. And he became very suspicious, and he wanted to uncover this wall.

speaker: interviewer
text: But there were Jewish policemen in the Warsaw Ghetto, some of whom were not very nice. They wanted to save their lives, you know, but they did not constitute a majority. I would say they constituted probably a minority. And this man's family, ironically enough, was hiding with us. He couldn't even save his family, you see? So he bought that Ukrainian. He gave him a watch or some gold, and he let us-- you know, he did not move away the wardrobe, the chifferobe.

speaker: interviewee
text: But-- let me just-- the houses and the buildings in Poland had a courtyard. I don't know if you're familiar with it. There's a courtyard and four buildings that, you know, it's a square.

speaker: interviewer
text: So an interior courtyard?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right, an interior courtyard. And in the building next to ours, in the courtyard-- we lived in the courtyard-- there was a family who was also hiding the way we were hiding, and they were discovered there. And they took them out, and they shot them all but for one boy, who was about eight years old. This is very significant, because I'll tell you something about it. They shot his parents in front of his eyes. And then, you know, we used to hide there all the time, whenever there was a raid, you know, whenever there was a roundup. And, ultimately, my father and two other men decided to build a bunker. And many people did. I mean, everybody was building bunkers in the cellars. And so the entrance to our bunker was through a toilet bowl. You moved-- I don't know, they cut away a part, and they were able to--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. We're going to-- before you get to the details--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --Of that, let's not miss any, and then we'll change the tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure. I just have a feeling that I'm so scattered.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's OK.
